ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002572); no specific Donne Biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Donne Biryani-specific code. A0FKP (biryani, rice dish with meat) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Donne Biryani or biryani as a specific food concept in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Chicken Donne Biryani is approximately 450 g, typically served in a traditional donne (areca palm leaf bowl) and includes rice, chicken pieces, and accompanying raita or salan.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for Chicken Biryani (rice + chicken + ghee + spices). Donne Biryani is a Karnataka-style variant using seeraga samba rice and higher ghee content. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Chicken Biryani entry with a modest upward adjustment to fat and energy to reflect the characteristic ghee-richness of Donne Biryani. Protein and carbohydrate values are held consistent with the ICMR-NIN base entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for Basmati/long-grain rice biryani
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Donne Biryani. Seeraga samba rice is a short-grain aromatic rice with a GI comparable to basmati rice. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 52–58 for basmati rice dishes and mixed rice-meat preparations. A GI of 54 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for Chicken Donne Biryani, consistent with the lower end of the biryani/basmati rice range. GL per serving calculated as: (54 × 20.5 × 4.5) / 100 = 49.9 g per 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1710 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving of Chicken Donne Biryani provides approximately 1710 mg sodium, representing over 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load per serving (~49.9 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of Chicken Donne Biryani is in the low range (GI 54), the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~92 g carbohydrate per 450 g) produces a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to approximately 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~2.9 g/100 g; ~13 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The generous use of ghee in Donne Biryani contributes approximately 13 g of saturated fat per standard serving, which exceeds the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-ghee preparation and limit serving frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Donne Biryani is a celebrated Karnataka-style biryani originating from Bangalore, India, distinguished by its use of seeraga samba rice — a short-grain, intensely aromatic variety — cooked with bone-in chicken pieces, generous quantities of ghee, whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise), fresh coriander, mint, green chillies, ginger, and garlic. The dish takes its name from the traditional donne, a bowl fashioned from areca palm leaves, in which it is served. Unlike Hyderabadi or Lucknowi dum biryani, Donne Biryani is cooked using a pressure-cooking or open-pot method that results in a moist, cohesive rice texture where the grains are slightly sticky and richly flavoured with meat juices and spice-infused ghee. The flavour profile is bold, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with a characteristic richness from ghee and a subtle sourness from yogurt used in the chicken marinade. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 788 kcal, 41 g protein, 31 g fat (of which ~13 g is saturated from ghee), and 92 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated (~1710 mg per serving), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is high due to the large rice portion, requiring careful management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, Chicken Donne Biryani is served at select South Indian restaurants and is sometimes offered only on specific days (e.g., Fridays), reflecting its status as a special-occasion dish.
common_user_queries: How is Chicken Donne Biryani different from regular biryani?
Is Donne Biryani available in Singapore?
What rice is used in Donne Biryani?
Why is it called Donne Biryani?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN provides a Chicken Biryani entry that is the closest approved primary source for Indian biryani. Donne Biryani is a regional Karnataka variant with higher ghee content; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN base with documented adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for basmati/seeraga samba rice biryani. No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for Donne Biryani specifically exists, justifying medium confidence.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Chicken Biryani source entry. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. 4. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Chicken Biryani (2017) with a modest upward adjustment to fat_g and energy_kcal to reflect the characteristic ghee-richness of Donne Biryani; all adaptations are documented in nutrient_source. 5. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for basmati rice and mixed rice-meat biryani preparations; seeraga samba rice has a comparable GI to basmati rice. 6. The food_name has been normalised to 'Chicken Donne Biryani' (removing the menu qualifier 'Only Fridays' which is a restaurant scheduling note, not part of the canonical food identity). 7. Halal flag is set to true as Donne Biryani is traditionally prepared with halal chicken and does not contain pork or alcohol; individual restaurant compliance should be verified. 8. Gluten-free flag is set to true as the dish is rice-based with no wheat ingredients; cross-contamination risk at restaurant level is not assessed here.

Set GI = 54 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for basmati/seeraga samba rice biryani; documented estimation basis.
